1.synthesis:
With garments specified, God now turns to the ceremony that would install Aaron and his sons in their priestly office. The ordination — the act of consecrating the priests — required a week-long series of sacrifices, washings, and anointings that are described here in careful detail. The opening verses catalogue the materials required: a young bull and two rams without blemish, unleavened bread, unleavened cakes mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers smeared with oil — all gathered in a basket and presented with the priests at the entrance to the tent of meeting (Exod 29:1-4).

Moses was to wash Aaron and his sons with water, clothe Aaron in the full high-priestly vestments described in ch 28, anoint him with oil, and then clothe his sons in tunics, sashes, and headbands (Exod 29:4-9). The anointing oil poured over Aaron's head (Exod 29:7) was so abundant that it ran down his beard and to the collar of his robes, as the poet of Psalm 133:2 would later celebrate as the paradigm of blessing and unity. The binding of the sash — the final act — concluded the investiture: "And the priesthood shall be theirs by a statute forever" (Exod 29:9).

Matthew Henry observes that the ceremony was "done with a great deal of ceremony and solemnity, that they themselves might be duly affected with the greatness of the work to which they were called, and that the people also might learn to magnify the office." The elaborate, week-long ritual was pedagogical as much as sacramental: it trained the people to understand that drawing near to God was no trivial matter, and that those who served as mediators bore a weight requiring extraordinary preparation.
1.historical_context: Ordination ceremonies for priests are attested across the ANE. Mesopotamian ritual texts describe the installation of temple personnel with washings, investitures, and sacrificial meals. Egypt's priestly induction also involved ritual purification and the donning of divine office symbols. What distinguishes Israel's ceremony is its explicit duration (seven days, mirroring the creation week), its blood-application to the priest's body, and its clear theological rationale: the priest approaches the holy God on behalf of sinners and must himself be atoned for. The ordination of Aaron and his sons at the base of Sinai took place in the first month of the second year after the Exodus, as the tabernacle was being erected (Lev 8 describes the actual execution of these instructions).
1.literary_structure: The ordination passage (vv 1-37) divides into preparation (vv 1-9), bull offering (vv 10-14), first ram offering (vv 15-18), second ram of ordination (vv 19-28), meal from the ordination offerings (vv 29-34), and seven-day duration summary (vv 35-37). The opening unit (vv 1-9) specifies what to gather and the washing-clothing-anointing sequence, establishing the logical order before detailing each sacrificial element.
1.theological_application: The washing (v 4) before clothing and anointing establishes the invariable sequence of approach to God: purification precedes service. The New Testament parallel is baptism — the washing of the new birth that precedes the believer's priestly service as part of the "royal priesthood" (1 Pet 2:9). Anointing with oil followed washing, speaking to the Holy Spirit's equipping that follows regeneration. The abundant oil of anointing poured over Aaron's head — running down to his garments — anticipates the Spirit poured out without measure on the anointed one of God, and then overflowing from him to all who belong to him (Acts 2:17).
1.christology: The ordination sequence — washed, clothed, anointed — is the template for Christ's own investiture in his threefold office. At his baptism in the Jordan, Jesus was washed in water, the heavens opened, and the Spirit descended on him "like a dove" (Matt 3:16) — the anointing for priestly, prophetic, and royal ministry. The voice from heaven — "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased" (Matt 3:17) — corresponds to the divine appointment of Aaron: "You shall consecrate them, that they may serve me as priests" (Exod 28:41). Christ's priestly office was eternal ("a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek," Ps 110:4), not hereditary — surpassing and superseding the Aaronic line whose ordination here foreshadowed him.

10.synthesis:
Three sacrificial acts formed the core of the priestly ordination: a sin offering with the bull, a burnt offering with the first ram, and the ram of ordination (the milluʾîm — "filling" or "installation") with the second. Each followed a distinct logic that together covered the whole spectrum of priestly need: atonement for sin, total consecration, and covenantal installation into office.

For the bull, Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head — a rite of identification and substitution (Exod 29:10). Adam Clarke explains: "By this rite the animal was consecrated to God, and was proper to be offered in sacrifice. Imposition of hands also signified that they offered the life of this animal as an atonement for their sins, and to redeem their lives from that death which, through their sinfulness, they had deserved." The bull's blood was applied to the altar's horns and poured out at its base; its fat, organs, and skin were burned outside the camp as a sin offering (Exod 29:11-14).

The first ram was a burnt offering — wholly consumed on the altar, ascending entirely to God as a sweet aroma (Exod 29:15-18). This total offering symbolized the total self-giving of the priest to divine service: nothing reserved, nothing held back. The second ram — the ram of ordination — added a remarkable new element: its blood was touched to the right ear, right thumb, and right great toe of Aaron and his sons (Exod 29:20), then sprinkled on the altar and upon the priests and their garments. The ear consecrated to hearing God's word, the thumb (hand) to doing God's work, the toe (foot) to walking in God's ways — the entire person claimed for sacred service.

The ordination concluded with a sacred meal: Moses's portion (the breast of wave offering) set aside, and Aaron and his sons eating the rest at the tabernacle entrance, the place of their installation (Exod 29:31-34). The meal sealed the covenant of ordination, and the seven-day repetition confirmed the thoroughness of the consecration — "so shall you do for seven days to make atonement for the altar" (Exod 29:37).
10.historical_context: The hand-laying (sāmakh) ritual for sacrificial animals appears throughout Leviticus as the standard rite of identification between offerer and sacrifice. It was not merely a dedication but a transfer: the worshipper's guilt was symbolically laid upon the animal that then died in his place. The three-part ordination offering (sin/burnt/installation) is unique to this ceremony — ordinary ordinations in the ANE did not require this full sequence. The blood application to the ear, hand, and foot parallels the cleansing rite for a healed leper (Lev 14:14-17), suggesting that the priest's entire person was being reclaimed from impurity for holy service — the priest "healed" of ordinary existence and consecrated to sacred function.
10.literary_structure: The three offerings follow the pattern: animal brought near → hands laid on → slaughtered → blood applied → appropriate portions designated and burned. The sin offering (vv 10-14) and burnt offering (vv 15-18) are the shorter units; the ordination ram (vv 19-34) receives the most detail because its blood-body-anointing rite is unique and its sacred meal concludes the ceremony. Verse 37 closes with the seven-day frame, binding the entire passage into a week-long consecration of both priests and altar.
10.theological_application: Adam Clarke's comment on the hand-laying captures the passage's central insight: the priest could not approach God without first transferring his guilt to a substitute. The sin offering before the burnt offering was not accidental — atonement must precede consecration. No one can give themselves wholly to God (burnt offering) without first having been cleansed of the guilt that disqualifies them (sin offering). Matthew Henry applies this to the gospel: "We must first receive the atonement before we can rejoice in God, or offer any spiritual sacrifice acceptable to him." The blood applied to ear, hand, and foot claims the whole person — hearing, working, walking — for divine service, a model for the New Testament call to "present your bodies as a living sacrifice" (Rom 12:1).
10.christology: The hand-laying on the sacrificial animals — the transfer of guilt to a substitute — is the anatomical gesture of the atonement. Isaiah 53 describes the same reality in prophetic poetry: "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" (Isa 53:6). The sin offering bull carried outside the camp and burned points directly to Christ, who "suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood" (Heb 13:12). The wave offering — the portion waved before God and then eaten by the priest in a sacred meal — foreshadows the Lord's Supper, where believers receive and eat the benefits of Christ's offering made before God on their behalf. The blood applied to ear, hand, and foot — claiming the priest's whole person — is fulfilled in Christ's own total consecration: he who "learned obedience through what he suffered" (Heb 5:8) is himself the perfectly obedient one, hearing the Father's voice completely, doing his work fully, walking his path without deviation.

38.synthesis:
Having specified the seven-day ordination ceremony, God turns to the daily rhythm that would sustain the sanctuary's ongoing life: two lambs, each a year old, offered every single day without exception — one in the morning and one at twilight (Exod 29:38-39). Each lamb was accompanied by a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter of a hin of beaten oil, and a quarter of a hin of wine as a drink offering (Exod 29:40). This was the tāmîd — the continual burnt offering — that would mark the rhythm of Israel's worship for centuries.

Keil and Delitzsch note that the two-lamb-per-day pattern was precisely calibrated: morning and evening, the two pivots of the day, bookending all of Israel's life with sacrifice. The sanctuary was never silent; its altar never cold; the smoke of the morning and evening burnt offerings rising day after day established the pattern that all Israel's prayer was to follow. By the time of Daniel, the interruption of the tāmîd had become the supreme sign of covenant desolation (Dan 11:31).

The theological rationale comes in verse 42-43: at the entrance to the tent of meeting, "I will meet with you, to speak with you there. There I will meet with the people of Israel, and it shall be sanctified by my glory." The daily offering was not a ritual duty performed in divine silence — it was the appointed occasion for God to speak to his people and to manifest his glory among them. The tāmîd was the divinely established appointment through which a relationship was maintained.

The passage closes with the great declaration of God's purpose: "I will dwell among the people of Israel and will be their God. And they shall know that I am the Lord their God, who brought them out of the land of Egypt that I might dwell among them. I am the Lord their God" (Exod 29:45-46). All the architecture, all the garments, all the sacrifices serve one end: the knowledge of God dwelling with his redeemed people.
38.historical_context: The daily sacrifice is one of the most attested institutions in Israel's later history. The Mishnah tractate Tamid is devoted entirely to the procedures of the morning and evening sacrifice in the Second Temple. The offering of two lambs per day, morning and evening, continued uninterrupted (when possible) from Sinai through the First Temple period and into the Second, until the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD. The cessation of the tāmîd under Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 167 BC was the precipitating atrocity that sparked the Maccabean revolt. The timing of the evening sacrifice — "between the two evenings" — structured Israel's day and became the time at which Jesus was crucified, the Passover lambs were slaughtered, and prayer was traditionally offered (Acts 3:1).
38.literary_structure: The daily offering (vv 38-46) follows the ordination instructions as a deliberate transition: from the one-time installation to the ongoing maintenance of priestly service. The passage ends with the theological summary of the entire Sinai building project (vv 45-46) — the dwelling of God among his redeemed people — making this paragraph the theological capstone of the entire instructions section that began in ch 25. The declaration "I am the Lord their God" (v 46) closes a bracket opened by "I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt" in the Decalogue (Exod 20:2).
38.theological_application:
Matthew Henry observes that the daily offering established the pattern of regular, unceasing worship: "The continual burnt-offering teaches us to offer up to God the spiritual sacrifices of prayer and praise every day, morning and evening." The tāmîd represents the principle that the covenant relationship is not maintained by occasional dramatic gestures but by daily faithfulness. The New Testament equivalent is the exhortation to "pray without ceasing" (1 Thess 5:17) and to offer "a sacrifice of praise to God continually" (Heb 13:15). The altar that was never cold, the smoke that never ceased — these are images of a life oriented toward God moment by moment.

The purpose statement of vv 45-46 is one of the clearest expressions of the covenant's goal in all of Scripture: God redeemed Israel from Egypt not primarily to give them land or law, but "that I might dwell among them." Redemption aims at fellowship; salvation is for the sake of relationship.
38.christology:
The morning and evening lamb — daily, without exception — is the most direct shadow of Christ in the entire Levitical system. John the Baptist's cry, "Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world" (John 1:29), identifies Jesus as the ultimate tāmîd: not two lambs per day, but one Lamb once for all. The tāmîd's perpetual smoke ascending to God from morning to evening corresponds to Christ's perpetual intercession — "he always lives to make intercession" (Heb 7:25) — for the continual maintenance of the covenant relationship between God and his people.

The declaration that God will "meet with you" at the place of the continual offering, and "speak with you there," anticipates John 14:6: Christ himself is the place of meeting, the one "through" whom all access to the Father flows. And the closing words — "I will dwell among the people of Israel" (Exod 29:45) — are the promise whose fulfillment John announces: "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:14), and whose final completion John of Patmos sees: "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them" (Rev 21:3).
